title: Fruit scones
subtitle: Sultana-studded afternoon-tea scones
excerpt: Twelve scones with a fine, even crumb and golden tops, made with cold butter rubbed in by hand and brought together with cold buttermilk in eight strokes. Sultanas swell into the crumb and give sweetness without sugar.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the basic scone formula: flour, butter, milk, a raising agent. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): the sultana addition and the no-twist cutter instruction.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked on the tray, bag once solid. Bake from frozen adding 3–4 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly to 24. Use two trays, swap shelves halfway through baking.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Rub the butter into the flour the night before and refrigerate the dry mix. Add liquid and sultanas the next morning.

glossaryTerms.definition: Working cold fat into flour with the fingertips until the mixture resembles fine breadcrumbs. The cold fat stays in discrete pieces, which steam during baking and create a flaky, tender crumb.

content.text: The scone rule that matters most is also the one most often ignored: bring the dough together in as few strokes as possible. Eight is the number. Ten is acceptable. Twenty is too many. Over-mixing develops the gluten and produces a tight, dense scone instead of the loose, layered one the recipe intends.
The other rule is the cutter: press straight down. No twist. A twist seals the cut edges together and prevents the scone from rising evenly.
Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Line the baking tray with baking paper.
Mix the {{self-raising-flour}}, {{fine-sea-salt}}, and {{baking-powder}} in a large bowl. Add the cold cubed {{unsalted-butter}} and
with cool fingertips until the mixture looks like fine breadcrumbs with no visible pieces of butter. Stir in the {{caster-sugar}} and {{sultanas}}.

Make a well in the centre and pour in the cold {{buttermilk}}. Use a palette knife or a flat butter knife to bring the dough together with cutting strokes — not stirring. Stop the moment there are no dry patches. The dough will be shaggy.

Turn the dough onto a lightly floured surface and pat it gently to 2.5 cm thick. Do not roll it repeatedly. Cut rounds with a 5 cm cutter dipped in flour, pressing straight down. Gather the offcuts, pat together once, and cut again. Arrange on the tray close together but not touching.

Brush the tops lightly with beaten egg. Take care not to let the glaze run down the sides, which would seal the layers and prevent the scone from rising evenly. Bake for 11–13 minutes, until the tops are deep gold and the scones have risen with a slight split around the middle. They are done when they sound slightly hollow tapped on the base.
Cool on the rack for at least 5 minutes before splitting. Serve with clotted cream and jam in whichever order you prefer.
